figures.caption: Figure 2: Service-oriented infrastructure components. (a) DCS coordinates topology discovery and weight metadata; clients abstract transport details; role nodes perform low-latency weight synchronization. (b) The health subsystem classifies role failures by severity and triggers either in-place or global restart accordingly.
Figure 6: Training-mode comparison on Qwen3-4B / DAPO-MATH-17k. (a) Reward by step. (b) Reward by wall-clock time. (c) Per-step time distribution. (d) Gantt-chart view of pipeline structure.

Figure 3: Timeline comparison of three training modes. (a) Colocate: rollout and training alternate on shared GPUs. (b) Separated on-policy: independent GPU groups with inter-batch synchronization. (c) Fully async off-policy: all groups run continuously with no blocking.
Figure 5: End-to-end performance: Relax vs. veRL on Qwen3-4B. (a) Per-step time comparison. (b) Gantt-chart view of pipeline overlap.
Table 1: End-to-end comparison: Relax vs. veRL on Qwen3-4B.

Figure 1: Relax system architecture. The Controller (control plane) orchestrates RL roles that execute on independent backends (computation plane) and exchange data exclusively through TransferQueue (data plane).
Figure 11: FP16 vs. BF16 precision ablation (Qwen3-4B). (a) Reward convergence. (b) Train–rollout log-probability absolute difference.
Figure 8: R3 ablation on Qwen3-30B-A3B: Relax vs. veRL. (a) Normalized routing mismatch. (b) Reward convergence. (c) Step time overhead (w/ R3 / w/o R3).
Figure 7: Colocate vs. fully async on Qwen3-Omni-30B / Echo Ink. (a) Reward by step. (b) Reward by wall-clock time.
Figure 4: Omni-modal reward convergence (Qwen3-Omni-30B). (a) Image+text+audio training on Echo Ink. (b) Video training on NextQA.
Figure 9: Text-only RL on DAPO-MATH (Qwen3-30B-A3B). (a) Training reward, pass@k, and response length. (b) AIME-24 evaluation pass@k over training steps.
Figure 10: Agentic RL reward convergence on multi-turn tool-calling (Qwen3-VL-MoE-30B, Deepeyes).
